Description: The module path is the canonical import path and should reflect where the module can be found by the go tool
Do: Use a domain you control as the module path prefix; use lowercase with no underscores
Dont: Don't use generic names like "myproject"; don't use uppercase or underscores in paths

Description: Go modules use semantic versioning and require a /vN suffix in the module path for major versions 2 and above
Do: Add /vN suffix to module path and import paths when releasing major version N >= 2; tag releases as vN.x.x
Dont: Don't change the module path suffix without a major version bump; don't make breaking changes in minor releases

Keywords: go.sum, checksum, verification, integrity, supply chain
Description: The go.sum file contains expected cryptographic hashes of module content to verify integrity and prevent tampering
Do: Commit go.sum to version control; run go mod verify in CI to check cached modules against go.sum
Dont: Don't add go.sum to .gitignore; don't manually edit go.sum; don't ignore checksum mismatch errors
Example: go mod verify returns all verified if checksums match or reports mismatches

Description: The replace directive substitutes the source of a module dependency with another location such as a local path or fork
Do: Use replace for temporary local development of dependencies; remove before committing if not intended as permanent
Dont: Don't leave replace directives pointing to local paths in published modules; don't use replace as a permanent fork strategy
Example: replace github.com/original/pkg => ../local-pkg, or replace github.com/original/pkg => github.com/yourfork/pkg v1.2.3

Description: Vendoring copies all dependencies into a vendor/ directory for fully reproducible builds without network access
Do: Use vendoring for production services that need hermetic builds; run go mod vendor and commit the vendor/ directory
Dont: Don't vendor libraries/SDKs intended for import by others; don't manually edit files inside vendor/
Example: go mod vendor && go build -mod=vendor ./..., commit vendor/ to VCS

Description: Private modules require configuring GOPRIVATE to bypass the public proxy and checksum database
Do: Set GOPRIVATE for your private domain; configure git credentials or .netrc for authentication; use GONOSUMDB if needed
Dont: Don't leave GOPRIVATE unset when using private repos; don't store credentials in source code
Example: GOPRIVATE=github.com/yourorg/* go mod tidy, or set in environment permanently

Keywords: mvs, version selection, dependency resolution, algorithm
Description: Go uses Minimal Version Selection to pick the minimum version of each dependency that satisfies all requirements
Do: Understand that Go picks the minimum not maximum version; explicitly require newer versions in go.mod if needed
Dont: Don't assume latest version is selected automatically; don't fight MVS by manually editing go.sum
Example: If A requires X v1.2.0 and B requires X v1.3.0 then Go selects X v1.3.0 as the minimum satisfying both

Description: The retract directive marks published versions as broken or inadvertently published so users avoid selecting them
Do: Use retract to flag accidentally published or broken versions; add a comment explaining the reason for retraction
Dont: Don't delete tags from VCS as a substitute for retract; don't retract versions without publishing a newer version first
Example: retract ( // Published accidentally v1.0.0 // Contains critical bug v1.1.0 )

Description: Workspace mode lets you work on multiple modules simultaneously without replace directives in go.mod files
Do: Use go work for local multi-module development; add go.work to .gitignore unless the workspace is part of the project
Dont: Don't commit go.work accidentally if it's only for local development; don't use workspaces in CI unless intentional
Example: go work init ./api ./sdk ./shared, then go work sync after dependency changes

Description: Regularly auditing and updating dependencies prevents security issues and keeps your project on supported versions
Do: Run go list -m -u all to check for updates; use govulncheck regularly; update dependencies incrementally
Dont: Don't update all dependencies at once without testing; don't ignore indirect dependency updates
Example: go list -m -u all to list updates, then go get -u specific/pkg@latest to update one at a time

Keywords: license, compliance, legal, open source, audit
Description: Checking licenses of all transitive dependencies ensures legal compliance especially for commercial software
Do: Use tools like go-licenses to audit dependency licenses; maintain a list of approved licenses for your organization
Dont: Don't ignore transitive dependency licenses; don't assume all open source licenses are compatible
Example: go-licenses csv ./... > licenses.csv, then review for copyleft or restricted licenses

Description: The module proxy caches and serves module downloads improving reliability and speed and providing immutability guarantees
Do: Use the default proxy (proxy.golang.org) for public modules; run a private proxy like Athens for private modules
Dont: Don't set GOPROXY=off unless you have vendored everything; don't bypass the proxy without understanding cache implications

Keywords: build tags, constraints, platform, conditional compilation
Description: Build tags control which files are included in a compilation allowing platform-specific or feature-gated code
Do: Use //go:build syntax (not // +build); organize platform-specific code in separate files with clear naming
Dont: Don't use build tags for configuration that should be runtime flags; don't forget the blank line after //go:build

Description: The go:embed directive embeds files and directories into the Go binary at compile time for self-contained deployment
Do: Use embed for templates and static assets and migration files; use embed.FS for directories
Dont: Don't embed large binary files unnecessarily; don't embed sensitive files like .env or credentials
Example: //go:embed templates/* var templateFS embed.FS, or //go:embed schema.sql var schema string

Keywords: tools, development, dependency, go generate, pin
Description: The tools.go pattern pins development tool dependencies in go.mod by importing them with a build tag that excludes normal builds
Do: Create a tools.go file with //go:build tools and blank imports of your tools; run go mod tidy to pin versions
Dont: Don't install tools globally when the project needs specific versions; don't forget the //go:build tools constraint
